\title{A Global 3\textdegree{} Periodic Structure in the UNESCO World Heritage\\
Longitude Distribution: Domed Monuments, the Babylonian Beru,\\
and a Levantine Harmonic Grid}

\author{Seth Tenenbaum\\
\small Independent Scholar\\
\small \href{mailto:sethtenenbaum1@gmail.com}{sethtenenbaum1@gmail.com}\\

\noindent\textcopyright~2026 Seth Tenenbaum.

Code: MIT License.
Data: UNESCO XML \textcopyright~UNESCO (research use); Wikidata extracts: CC0.

\begin{abstract}
This exploratory study documents a global 3\textdegree{} periodic structure
in the UNESCO World Heritage Cultural and Mixed longitude distribution
($N = \NclusterTotal$). No timestamped pre-registration was filed, and all
results are treated as exploratory.

Every longitude of the form $x.18$\textdegree{}E (120 equally spaced anchors)
yields the maximum observed count of sites within 6.7~km of a harmonic node
(Tier-A+, against a 4\% geometric null). The 3\textdegree{} spacing equals
0.1~beru, the smallest integer sub-division of the Babylonian angular unit
(MUL.APIN, c.~1000~BCE). Gerizim's longitude falls within this phase band,
initiating the analysis (\S\ref{sec:circularity}).

Domed and spherical monuments ($N = \NcircTotal$) show \NcircApRate\%
Tier-A+ (permutation $Z = \simDomePermZ$, $p = \simDomePermP$;
Bonferroni $p_\mathrm{adj} = \pAdjTestTwo$, $k = 2$).
A+-bearing harmonics host $\clusterHarmonicRatio\times$ more total sites
than non-A+-bearing harmonics (permutation $p = \clusterPermP$).
Leave-one-out checks confirm no single site drives either result.

Geographic concentration of Eurasian civilizations in longitude bands
aligned with beru harmonics cannot be excluded on this corpus alone.
The dome enrichment survives all three simulation nulls and exceeds the
expectation from monuments at the same longitude footprint
(Null~C, \S\ref{sec:geo_conc_null}), but the within-dome bootstrap
confirms that domed monuments' own longitudes encode the enrichment
(Null~A, ns). The primary contribution is documentation of a reproducible
quantitative regularity. The metrological interpretation awaits independent
historical confirmation.
\end{abstract}

\noindent\textbf{Keywords:}
longitude periodicity, monument distribution, UNESCO World Heritage,
Babylonian beru, ancient units of measurement, domed architecture,
stupa, spatial statistics, binomial enrichment test, heritage site geography,
archaeometry, Levantine metrology, quantitative archaeology, Mount Gerizim

\newpage

% ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
% §1 Introduction
% ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

\section{Introduction}
\label{sec:introduction}

The UNESCO World Heritage Cultural and Mixed corpus ($N = \NclusterTotal$)
appears to contain a global 3\textdegree{} periodic structure in its
longitude distribution.
Every longitude of the form $x.18$\textdegree{}E produces the maximum
observed Tier-A+ count when used as an angular reference (sites within
$\lesssim 6.7$~km of a harmonic node, against a 4\% geometric null).
The 3\textdegree{} spacing equals the smallest integer sub-division of
the Babylonian \textit{beru} unit
(0.1~beru~$= 3$\textdegree{}~$= 3$~\textit{u\v{s}}, MUL.APIN, c.~1000~BCE).
The analytic consequences of the Gerizim phase-coincidence
that initiated this study are defined in \S\ref{sec:circularity}.

\subsection{Circularity Containment Protocol}
\label{sec:circularity}

This subsection is the single canonical statement of the circularity problem
and its analytic consequences. All later mentions are back-references to
this section.

\textbf{How the circularity arose.}
This study began with the hypothesis that the Babylonian beru unit might be
detectable as a longitude-spacing signal in the UNESCO corpus, using Mount
Gerizim (\GerizimLon\textdegree{}E) as the reference anchor.
Running the global anchor sweep revealed that Gerizim's longitude
falls within the $x.18$\textdegree{}E phase band.
If the sweep had revealed a different optimal phase, this
paper would not exist in its current form.

\textbf{What the circularity contaminates.}
\begin{itemize}
  \item The Levantine corridor statistics (Tests~1, 2, 3 as computed from
    Gerizim), since reporting them was conditioned on observing the
    phase alignment.
  \item The $x.18$\textdegree{}E framing, which was identified in the sweep,
    not predicted independently.
  \item The Rayleigh Test~B, whose near-perfect $R$ follows mathematically
    from the A+ classification criterion (\S\ref{sec:results_rayleigh}).
  \item All narrative emphasis on the Levantine corridor as historically
    motivated.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{What the circularity does \emph{not} contaminate.}
\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{The existence of a 3\textdegree{} periodic structure in the
    UNESCO longitude file.}
    Any investigator running an unanchored 120-phase scan will recover it
    without reference to Gerizim.
  \item \emph{The dome enrichment (Test~2).}
    All 120 $x.18$\textdegree{}E anchors produce identical A+ counts for
    the dome population.
  \item \emph{The cluster asymmetry (Test~3).}
    This is a full-corpus result requiring no keyword filter.
  \item \emph{The unit sensitivity sweep.}
    The sharp collapse at $\pm 0.3\%$ of the canonical spacing is a
    property of the corpus structure, not of the choice of anchor.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{The protocol.}
\emph{This identification problem cannot be closed by any statistical method
applied to this corpus.} There is no way to demonstrate after the fact
whether the $x.18$\textdegree{}E phase was discovered before or after
Gerizim was chosen as the anchor; even a time-stamped archive cannot resolve
this because the internal sequence of analysis steps is unverifiable by any
external party.
Accordingly:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
  \item All results are treated as \emph{exploratory} without exception.
  \item The Bonferroni correction is applied as a \emph{descriptive bound} on
    effect magnitude.
  \item The Levantine corridor statistics are reported as the motivated entry
    point, not as independent evidence.
  \item Downstream sections back-reference this section rather than re-stating
    the argument.
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Why this design avoids the main pitfalls of archaeoastronomy.}
Prior metrological investigations were rightly criticized for investigator-assembled
site lists \citep{williamson1983leylines,freeman1976,kendall1974}.
Three features distinguish this study. First, the UNESCO site list was compiled
without reference to this hypothesis and is fixed. Second, the angular interval
(0.1~beru) was selected on metrological grounds, not tuned post-hoc. Third,
tier boundaries and keyword sets were specified before any beru distances were
computed.

\textbf{Structure of the paper.}
Section~\ref{sec:prior} provides background on the beru unit.
Section~\ref{sec:methods} describes data, classification, and multiple-comparisons protocol.
Section~\ref{sec:results} presents results.
Section~\ref{sec:robustness} reports robustness checks.
Section~\ref{sec:limitations} catalogs limitations.
Section~\ref{sec:discussion} evaluates alternative explanations.
The Appendix contains the keyword audit.

%──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\section{Background}
\label{sec:prior}
%──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

\subsection{The Beru Unit}
\label{sec:beru}

The \textit{beru} (30\textdegree{} of arc) is attested in the MUL.APIN
cuneiform compendium \citep{hunger2019mul,watson2011writing}, dated on broad
grounds to the late second or early first millennium~BCE. In this system the
unit divided the ecliptic into twelve equal segments and served for both stellar
and terrestrial angular measurement. The terrestrial use is attested explicitly
in MUL.APIN~II~ii~43--iii~15, which converts beru of daylight into ground
distances \citep[pp.~2--15]{hunger2019mul}. The sub-division into
30~\textit{u\v{s}} (1~u\v{s}~$= 1$\textdegree{}) is well attested
\citep{hunger2019mul,robson2008mathematics}, and the 0.1-beru interval
(3\textdegree{}) tested here corresponds to exactly 3~\textit{u\v{s}}.

The beru also functioned as a unit of territorial description. Royal
inscriptions record inter-regional distances in beru between major cities
\citep{neugebauer1975history,robson2008mathematics}. On the Babylonian
\textit{Imago Mundi}, distances between the ``islands'' beyond the encircling
Salt Sea are marked as multiples of beru. The \textit{kudurru} (boundary stone)
tradition records territorial extents in cuneiform, and many kudurru were
deposited in temples as legally authoritative records
\citep{robson2008mathematics,powell1987metrological}. This combination of a
fractional angular unit at cosmological scales and temple-deposited territorial
records provides the documentary basis for testing whether a 0.1-beru harmonic
spacing is detectable in the longitude distribution of major monuments. This
study tests the statistical pattern only. The mechanism by which any such
correspondence could arise is not documented and is outside the scope of this
paper.

\subsection{Ancient Metrology and Geodetic Practice}

Whether pre-modern societies employed standardized angular measures
across large geographic scales has a long and contested history.
Thom's ``megalithic yard'' \citep{thom1967,thom1971,thom1964}
stimulated statistical refinement \citep{kendall1974} and methodological
critique \citep{freeman1976}. Powell's treatment of Mesopotamian metrology
\citep{powell1987metrological} remains the standard reference. More recently,
Magli \citep{magli2013,magli2016} has applied Monte Carlo methods to
archaeoastronomical alignments, and Ruggles and Silva have advanced
probability-based frameworks for evaluating orientation claims
\citep{ruggles1999astronomy,ruggles2015handbook,silva2020statistical}.
The ICOMOS-IAU thematic study \citep{ruggles2010icomos} provides
institutional context for archaeoastronomical analysis of World Heritage
properties.

This study departs from prior work in three respects. First, the site list
is externally curated (the UNESCO World Heritage List), eliminating selection
bias. Second, the test is one-dimensional (longitude only), yielding a
well-defined geometric null (4\%) with no free parameters. Third, the
analysis is fully computational and publicly archived \citep{tenenbaum2026audit}.

\subsection{The Anchor: Mount Gerizim}
\label{sec:anchor}

The circularity arising from Gerizim's longitude coinciding with the optimal
phase band is defined in \S\ref{sec:circularity}.

Mount Gerizim (\GerizimLon\textdegree{}E) is the primary reference
anchor because of its documented \textit{tabbur ha-aretz} (``navel of the
earth'') role in Samaritan cosmographic tradition
\citep{purvis1968,macdonald1964,crown1989}, analogous to Babylon's meridian
in cuneiform astronomical records \citep{hunger2019mul}. No optimization
across alternative Levantine anchors was performed.

Gerizim's tentative-list coordinate (ref.~5706, DMS \GerizimLonDMS{}) is
externally sourced and does not appear in the UNESCO inscribed corpus.%
\footnote{UNESCO World Heritage Centre, \textit{Mount Gerizim and the
Samaritans}, Tentative List ref.~5706 (State of Palestine, submitted
02~April~2012; criteria (iii)(vi); coordinates N~32\textdegree{}12'44''
E~35\textdegree{}16'08''). \url{https://whc.unesco.org/en/tentativelists/5706/}.
Archived rendered copy:
\url{https://ia601602.us.archive.org/33/items/unesco_5706_rendered/unesco_5706_rendered.html}.}
Jerusalem (35.2317\textdegree{}E), approximately \GerizimJerusalemKm~km east
of Gerizim, is an inscribed corpus member. It is self-excluded when used as
anchor and produces the same A+ count (\S\ref{sec:sensitivity_global}).
The enrichment belongs to the \GerizimJerusalemKm~km corridor, not to the
specific choice of anchor within it.
Mecca reaches only $p \approx 0.057$ (ns).

\subsection{Interpretive Framework}
\label{sec:framework}

This paper is a pattern-detection study, not a historical attribution.
Three classes of explanation are evaluated throughout.

\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{Geographic concentration.}
Major civilizations that produced UNESCO-ranked monuments developed in
Eurasian longitude bands that align with 0.1-beru harmonics.
The dedicated geographic-concentration simulation (\S\ref{sec:geo_conc_null})
shows this remains a viable first-level account.

\item \textbf{Metrological transmission.}
A 3\textdegree{} (0.1-beru) angular reference system anchored near the Levant
informed the placement of major foundations. No mechanism is documented.

\item \textbf{Unmodeled statistical artifact.}
A systematic bias in UNESCO curation, geocoding, or the discrete longitude
distribution produces the apparent periodicity. An artifact account must
additionally explain the dome enrichment and the cluster asymmetry.
\end{enumerate}

Adjudicating between explanations~1 and~2 requires independent corpora.

%──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\section{Data and Methods}
\label{sec:methods}
%──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

\subsection{Geodetic Framework}

The reference anchor is Mount Gerizim at \GerizimLon\textdegree{}E,
from Palestine's UNESCO tentative list (ref.~5706, 2012).\footnote{See
footnote in \S\ref{sec:anchor} for full citation and archived URL.}
Gerizim is not on the inscribed World Heritage List and does not appear
in the test-site corpus. Jerusalem is also excluded in both sweeps (\S\ref{sec:anchor}).

For a site at longitude $\lambda$, the beru-distance is
$B = |\lambda - \GerizimLon| / 30$ and the beru-deviation from the
nearest 0.1-beru harmonic is
$\delta = |B - \operatorname{round}(B, 0.1)|$.
The tier classification is a fixed angular criterion.
Kilometer labels (e.g.\ $\lesssim 6.7$~km for Tier-A+) are equatorial
approximations computed as $\delta \times 30 \times 111$~km/\textdegree{},
becoming smaller at higher latitudes. This makes the angular criterion
\emph{conservative} at high latitudes. A geodesic sensitivity check confirms
that applying a strict physical threshold would increase the A+ count to
\GeodesicApPhysical{}, strengthening all primary results. No latitude
correction is applied.

The geometric null for Tier-A+ is $2 \times 0.002 / 0.1 = 4\%$,
assuming uniform longitudes. This serves as a convenient reference, but
the primary tests are simulation-based null models (\S\ref{sec:simulation_null})
that preserve the empirical longitude distribution. A Gaussian KDE produces
$\simKDEMean \pm \simKDEStd$ expected A+ ($\sim$4.0\%), confirming the
geometric null is not materially inflated by longitude clumping.

\subsection{Metrological Basis for the 0.1-Beru Spacing}
\label{sec:metrology_basis}

The harmonic spacing tested is 0.1~beru (3\textdegree{}), the smallest integer
sub-division of the beru attested in cuneiform metrology, equalling exactly
3~\textit{u\v{s}} \citep{hunger2019mul,robson2008mathematics,powell1987metrological}.
It was selected because it is the finest metrologically attested integer
sub-division that yields a tractable test (120 harmonics, 4\% geometric null).
Coarser spacings (0.5~beru, 1~beru) are uninformative; finer sub-divisions
(0.02~beru) do not produce the signal (Table~\ref{tab:unitsweep}).
No spacing was selected post-hoc to maximize the result.

The 0.05-beru spacing (6\textdegree{}) also reaches significance
(Table~\ref{tab:unitsweep}). This is structurally expected because every
0.10-beru harmonic is also a 0.05-beru harmonic, making the 0.05-beru
result a coarser sub-sampling of the same periodicity, not a separate signal.

\subsection{Tier Classification (fixed before data analysis)}

Sites are classified by $\delta$, the angular deviation from the
nearest 0.1-beru harmonic. All tier boundaries and test populations were
defined before any beru values were computed.

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Tier A++}: $\delta \leq 0.0002$~beru ($\lesssim 0.67$~km).
    $P_0 = 0.4\%$. Descriptive only, not tested.
  \item \textbf{Tier A+}: $\delta \leq 0.002$~beru ($\lesssim 6.7$~km).
    $P_0 = 4\%$. Primary test threshold.\footnote{$2 \times 0.002 / 0.1 = 4\%$.
    Km labels are equatorial approximations.}
  \item \textbf{Tier A}: $\delta \leq 0.010$~beru ($\lesssim 33$~km).
    $P_0 = 20\%$.
  \item \textbf{Tier B}: $\delta \leq 0.050$~beru.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Domed and Spherical Monuments (Test 2)}
\label{sec:methodscircular}

\NcircKeywords{} morphological keywords from four roots
(\textit{stupa/stupas}, \textit{tholos}, \textit{dome/domed/domes},
\textit{spherical}) are matched as whole words against each site's
name, XML description, and extended OUV text.
All keywords are treated identically (positive whole-word match, no context
gating), making the population mechanically determined by the keyword list.
Final population: $N = \NcircTotal$.

A context-validated robustness check (Test~2x) applies sentence-level
architectural confirmation, reducing the population to $N = 83$
(\NcircContextRejected{} sites rejected, e.g.\ Uluru ``rock domes,''
Hiroshima ``Genbaku Dome,'' full list in Appendix~\ref{sec:kwdaudit_dome}).
The validated version yields $p = 0.0005$, $3.31\times$ enrichment, stronger
than the raw sweep ($p = 0.0009$, $3.06\times$), consistent with rejected
sites being non-architectural false positives that dilute the signal.
Because the validation rules were developed after the initial sweep,
Test~2x is a post-hoc robustness check excluded from the primary
Bonferroni family.

\subsection{Hemispherical Mound Evolution (Test 2b [Sensitivity Variant])}
\label{sec:methodsevolution}

Extends Test~2 by adding earthen mound-stage keywords
(\textit{tumulus, tumuli, barrow, barrows, kofun, mound}).
The categories share hemispherical or quasi-hemispherical geometry
associated with cosmological and funerary symbolism across cultures.
They are not grouped on the basis of architectural lineage.

Test~2b is classified as a \emph{sensitivity variant} of Test~2, not an
independent hypothesis. Its population shares the entire dome/stupa core
with Test~2 and adds only \NevoMoundOnly{} mound-stage sites, which show no
significant enrichment on their own (\evoMoundApRate\%, $p = \pEvoMound$, ns).
A context-validated version (Test~2bx) reduces the population to $N = 126$
with stronger results ($p = 0.0005$, $2.78\times$).

\subsection{Cluster Asymmetry (Test 3)}

Full corpus ($N = \NclusterTotal$), no keyword filter.
For each site, count other UNESCO sites within $\pm 0.3$\textdegree{}
longitude. Compare cluster sizes for A+ vs.\ non-A+ sites (Mann-Whitney $U$,
permutation test). At the harmonic level, compare per-harmonic site counts
for A+-bearing vs.\ non-A+ harmonics.

\subsection{Founding-Site Enrichment (Test E [Exploratory])}
\label{sec:methodsfounding_origin}

Two independent keyword analyses on the full corpus ($N = \NoriginCorpus$),
no keyword filtering for the primary tests:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{World religion founding sites.}
    Keyword match for Buddhism, Christianity, Islam, Judaism,
    Hinduism, Zoroastrianism in site name/XML description.
  \item \textbf{UNESCO founding language.}
    Sites where UNESCO uses ``first capital,'' ``birthplace of,''
    ``founded in,'' etc.\ in the XML description.
\end{enumerate}
A context-aware keyword classifier
(\texttt{lib/founding\_filter.py}) was applied to all sites.
This classifier was iteratively tuned after examining misclassified
sites (full disclosure in \S\ref{sec:filter_methodology}). Because
refinement occurred after data inspection, the enrichment result
from this classifier should be treated as \emph{Exploratory}.
The inscription-year gradient and world-religion sub-tests do not
depend on this classifier.

\subsection{Temporal Gradient (Test 4)}
\label{sec:methodstemporal}

Cochran-Armitage trend test on the full corpus ($N = \NoriginCorpus$),
no keyword filter.
A+ rate is tested for a monotonic decline as UNESCO inscription year
increases (earliest inscriptions reflect greatest site importance).
Three-cohort grouping (1978--1984, 1985--1999, 2000--2025) is primary;
five-cohort grouping (1978--1984, 1985--1991, 1992--1999, 2000--2009,
2010--2025) is secondary.
The three-cohort bins are formed by merging the two middle five-cohort
bins (1985--1991 and 1992--1999) into a single 1985--1999 bin.
Code: \texttt{analysis/unesco/temporal\_gradient\_test.py}.

\textbf{Note on the regional composition confound.}
The founding canon (1978--1984) is concentrated in Europe and the
Mediterranean corridor, the Eurasian heartland where the beru-harmonic
signal is strongest.
Later cohorts add proportionally more sites from Africa, the Americas,
and Oceania, regions where the null hypothesis predicts lower A+ rates
regardless of any metrological effect.
A within-region diagnostic (\texttt{regional\_temporal\_gradient.py})
finds that \stratNconcordant{} of \stratNregions{} regions show the
early greater than late direction, but \stratNsig{} reach individual
significance, and the diagnostic is underpowered at $N = \stratNregions$ regions.
The Mantel-Haenszel region-adjusted OR is $\MHcommonOR$ ($p = \pCMH$).
The temporal gradient is therefore treated as descriptive only; it
indicates that UNESCO's earliest inscription cohort is concentrated
in the very regions where the beru-harmonic signal is strongest,
but does not establish that time itself, independent of geography,
drives the pattern.

\subsection{Multiple-Comparisons Protocol}
\label{sec:bonferroni}

All results are exploratory (\S\ref{sec:circularity}).

\textbf{Primary pre-specified family ($k = 2$).}
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Test 2} (domed/spherical monuments, $N = \NcircTotal$):
    morphological sub-population enriched above 4\% null.
  \item \textbf{Test 3} (cluster asymmetry, full corpus, no keyword filter):
    A+-bearing harmonics host more total sites.
\end{itemize}
These address structurally different questions on different populations.
Both pass the Bonferroni bound at $k = 2$
(dome $p_\mathrm{adj} = \pAdjTestTwo$~***,
cluster $p_\mathrm{adj} = \pAdjTestThree$~**)
and the conservative $k = 5$ bound
(dome $p_\mathrm{adj} = 0.005$~***,
cluster $p_\mathrm{adj} = 0.017$~**).

\textbf{Other tests.}
Test~1 (full corpus) is descriptive and shares A+ sites with Test~3.
Test~2b is a sensitivity variant of Test~2.
Test~4 (temporal gradient) is descriptive only after within-region
adjustment (\S\ref{sec:resultstemporal}).
Test~E (founding classifier) is fully post-hoc (Appendix~\ref{sec:kwdaudit_founding}).
Test~B (Rayleigh) is a structural description (\S\ref{sec:results_rayleigh}).

Test~4 (temporal)      & \NoriginCorpus  & \pCochranThree  & \pAdjTestFour      & ---               & Descriptive \\
Test~E (founding)      & \NoriginCorpus  & \pFoundKwFisher & N/A                & N/A               & Post-hoc \\
Test~B (Rayleigh)$^\dagger$ & \Nap       & \pRawTestB      & N/A                & N/A               & Structural \\
\bottomrule
\multicolumn{7}{l}{\footnotesize $^\dagger$Test~B: $R\approx1$ by A+ construction; excluded from all family counts (\S\ref{sec:results_rayleigh}).} \\
\end{tabular}}
\end{table}

A comprehensive FDR audit (\NtotalTests{} tests,
\citealt{benjamini1995controlling}) retains \NsurviveFDR{} at $q < 0.05$,
of which \NsurviveBonfAll{} survive Bonferroni at $k = \NtotalTests$
(Table~\ref{tab:fdr_full}).

\subsection{Keyword Filter Methodology}
\label{sec:filter_methodology}

The dome/stupa filter required minimal iteration and was fixed before
beru computations. The founding-site filter (\texttt{lib/founding\_filter.py})
was developed iteratively by examining misclassified sites. The founding
enrichment result ($p = \pFoundKwFisher$) is directly affected by this
tuning. The two primary results (Tests~2 and~3) and all keyword-independent
sub-tests are unaffected by the founding classifier. Full keyword lists and
per-site audit trails are in Appendix~\ref{sec:kwdaudit}.

\subsection{Pre-Registration}
\label{sec:prereg}

No timestamped pre-registration was filed. All results are exploratory
(\S\ref{sec:circularity}). The entire analysis pipeline is publicly
archived \citep{tenenbaum2026audit}.

\subsection{Null Model Validation}
\label{sec:methods_simulation}

Three simulation approaches (code: \texttt{simulation\_null\_model.py},
100{,}000 trials each): (1)~longitude permutation, (2)~bootstrap from
empirical distribution, (3)~KDE-smoothed null. Results in
\S\ref{sec:simulation_null}.

As an independent control, the UNESCO corpus is compared against
the Wikidata P1435 global heritage corpus ($N = \WDctrlN$).
Code: \texttt{data/scripts/fetch\_p1435\_global.py}.

%──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\section{Results}
\label{sec:results}

Results are presented in order: Test~2 (dome/spherical, primary),
Test~3 (cluster asymmetry, primary), Test~2b (sensitivity variant),
Test~1 (full corpus, descriptive), and Test~4 (temporal gradient, descriptive).
Test~B (Rayleigh) is a structural description, not independent evidence
(\S\ref{sec:results_rayleigh}).
Test~E (founding classifier) is in Appendix~\ref{sec:kwdaudit_founding}.

%──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\subsection{Domed and Spherical Monuments (Test~2, Primary)}
\label{sec:resultscircular}

The \NcircKeywords-keyword morphological filter yields $N = \NcircTotal$ sites.
Primary tests: permutation $Z = \simDomePermZ$,
$p = \simDomePermP$; bootstrap $p = \simDomeBootP$.
The context-validated check (Test~2x, $N = 83$) yields an equal-or-stronger
signal ($p = 0.0005$, $3.31\times$).

\begin{table}[H]
\centering
\caption{UNESCO domed/spherical monument test ($N = \NcircTotal$, raw keyword sweep).
  Primary evidence against the chance explanation: simulation permutation
  $Z = \simDomePermZ$, $p = \simDomePermP$ (\S\ref{sec:simulation_null}).
  Exact binomial results are reference baselines assuming uniform longitude
  spacing. The non-significant $\chi^2$ is discussed in the text.}
\label{tab:circularstats}
\adjustbox{max width=\textwidth}{%
\begin{tabular}{lrrrrrl}
\toprule
Metric & Obs & Exp($H_0$) & Rate [95\% CI] & Enrich. & $p$ & \\
\midrule
Tier-A+ ($\leq 0.002$ beru) & \NcircTierAp & \circExpAp & \NcircApRate\% [\circApCIlo, \circApCIhi] & $\circEnrichAp\times$ & \pCircAp & *** \\
Tier-A\phantom{+} ($\leq 0.010$ beru)  & \NcircTierA  & \circExpA & \circARate\% [\circACIlo, \circACIhi] & $\circEnrichA\times$ & \pCircA  & ** \\
$\chi^2$-uniform (5 bins, df\,=\,4) & --- & --- & ---  & --- & \pCircChi & ns \\
\midrule
\multicolumn{7}{l}{Expected under $H_0$: A+ = 4\%, A = 20\%. CIs are Clopper-Pearson exact.} \\
\multicolumn{7}{l}{Tier-B: \NcircTierB\,/\,\NcircTotal\phantom{0} \ \ Mean $\delta$: \circMeanDev\,beru} \\
\multicolumn{7}{l}{\footnotesize *** $p < 0.001$, ** $p < 0.01$, * $p < 0.05$, $^\dagger$ $p < 0.10$ (trend-level), ns = not significant.} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}}% end adjustbox
\end{table}

\noindent\textbf{Tier-A+ sites} ($N = \NcircTierAp$, \NcircApRate\%, $\circEnrichAp\times$,
permutation $p = \simDomePermP$,
Bonferroni $p_\mathrm{adj} = \pAdjTestTwo$, $k = 2$):
Khoja Ahmed Yasawi (0.2~km),
Boyana Church (0.3~km),
Lumbini (0.8~km),
Humayun's Tomb (2.0~km),
Pyu Ancient Cities (2.3~km),
Silk Roads: Chang'an-Tianshan (2.5~km), Trulli of Alberobello (3.6~km),
Old City of Jerusalem (4.1~km),
Borobudur (4.3~km; \citealt{miksic1990borobudur}),
Kizhi Pogost (5.0~km), Echmiatsin/Zvartnots (6.2~km).

A leave-one-out check confirms no single site drives the result:
removing any A+ site yields $N = \LOOdomeN$, A+$= \LOOdomeAp$,
$p = \LOOdomeP$.
Leave-2-out (worst case across all $\binom{\LKOnAp}{2}$ combinations):
$p = \LKOworstTwoP$.
Leave-3-out (worst case): $p = \LKOworstThreeP$.
Code: \texttt{dome\_leave\_k\_out.py}.

\textbf{Geographic-concentration null (dedicated simulation).}
\label{sec:geo_conc_null}
Domed monuments are concentrated in the Eurasian corridor:
\domeEurasianFraction\% fall in 20\textdegree--120\textdegree{}E
versus \fullCorpusEurasianFraction\% of the full corpus.
Two targeted null models test whether this explains the dome enrichment
(code: \texttt{dome\_geographic\_concentration\_test.py}, 100{,}000 trials).

\textbf{Null~A (within-dome bootstrap):} Draw $N = \NcircValidated$ longitudes
from the dome sites' own list. Mean $= \geoNullDomeBootMean$ A+,
$p = \geoNullDomeBootP$ (ns). The dome sites' positions are concentrated
near harmonic longitudes.

\textbf{Null~C (restricted geographic draw):} Pool all
\geoNullDomeRestrictedN{} full-corpus sites within $\pm 5$\textdegree{} of
any dome site. Pool mean $= \geoNullDomeRestrictedMean$ A+ (5.8\%),
$p = \geoNullDomeRestrictedP$ (**). Dome sites (11 A+, 13.3\%) significantly
exceed the broader footprint. Sensitivity at $\pm 2$\textdegree{} and
$\pm 10$\textdegree{} yields consistent results.

\textbf{Reconciliation.}
Null~A confirms that the dome sites' longitude distribution reproduces their
enrichment (geographic concentration is necessary). Null~C establishes that
other monuments at the same longitudes are significantly less enriched
(geographic concentration is not sufficient). The residual question is whether
the sub-longitude precision of domed monument placement reflects metrological
intent or an unmodeled aspect of these civilizations. Adjudicating requires
independent corpora.

In the global anchor sweep (\S\ref{sec:sensitivity_global}),
Gerizim scores \GerizimSweepAp{} A+ (\anchorSweepPctile{}\textsuperscript{th}
percentile); Jerusalem scores \JerusalemSweepAp{}
(\JerusalemSweepPctile{}\textsuperscript{th}).
The meta-keyword test confirms that only \metaBeatingChiPct\% of
\NmetaKeywords{} candidate words achieve comparable enrichment.

\subsection{Hemispherical Mound Evolution (Test 2b [Sensitivity Variant])}
\label{sec:resultsevolution_inline}

The combined hemispherical population ($N = \NevoTotal$) comprises
\NevoMoundOnly{} tumulus/barrow/kofun sites added to the core domed/spherical
population ($N = \NcircTotal$), plus \NevoOverlap{}
sites matching both mound and dome/stupa keywords.
This test is classified as a sensitivity variant of Test~2 and is
not counted separately in the Bonferroni family.

\noindent The combined population ($N = \NevoTotal$, A+ = \evoApRate\%,
$p = \pEvoAp$~***) is consistent with Test~2. The mound stage does not reach
significance (\evoMoundApRate\%, $p = \pEvoMound$, ns). The signal is carried
by the stupa ($5.56\times$, $p = \pEvoStupa$~**) and dome sub-stages.
Leave-one-out checks confirm stability for all sub-stages.

\textbf{Stupa geographic-concentration null.}
\label{sec:stupa_geo_null}
Stupa sites are concentrated in South/Southeast Asia
(\stupaHeartlandFrac\% in 70\textdegree--110\textdegree{}E).
Null~A (within-stupa bootstrap): $p = \stupaGeoBootP$ (ns).
Null~C (restricted $\pm 5$\textdegree{} draw): $p = \stupaGeoRestrictedP$ (*).
The structure parallels the dome result: geographic proximity to harmonic
longitudes is necessary but not sufficient to explain the morphological
specificity.

\subsection{Cluster Asymmetry (Test~3, Primary Pre-specified)}
\label{sec:resultscluster}

\begin{table}[H]
\centering
\caption{Cluster asymmetry: A+ vs.\ non-A+ sites in the full UNESCO corpus
  ($N = \NclusterTotal$; cluster radius $\pm 0.3$\textdegree{} lon)}
\label{tab:clusterasym}
\small
\adjustbox{max width=\textwidth}{%
\begin{tabular}{p{6.2cm}rrrr}
\toprule
Metric & A+ sites & Non-A+ sites & Ratio / $Z$ & $p$ \\
\midrule
Mean cluster size (sites within $\pm 0.3$\textdegree{} lon)
  & \clusterApMean & \clusterNonApMean & \clusterRatio$\times$ & \\
Mann-Whitney $U$ (one-tailed)
  & & & & \clusterMWp~* \\
Permutation (10{,}000 shuffles)
  & & & $Z = \clusterPermZ$ & \clusterPermP~** \\
\midrule
\multicolumn{5}{l}{\textit{Harmonic-level density (all Tier $\leq$ B sites per 0.1-beru harmonic):}} \\
Harmonics with $\geq 1$ A+ site (\NclusterApHarmonics{})
  & \multicolumn{2}{c}{mean $= \clusterHarmonicApMean$ sites}
  & & \\
Harmonics with 0 A+ sites (\NclusterNonApHarmonics{})
  & \multicolumn{2}{c}{mean $= \clusterHarmonicNonApMean$ sites}
  & \clusterHarmonicRatio$\times$ & $< 0.0001$~*** \\
\midrule
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}}% end adjustbox
\end{table}

\noindent A+-bearing harmonics host \clusterHarmonicRatio$\times$ more sites
on average than non-A+ harmonics ($p < 0.0001$).
Sites at harmonic nodes cluster more densely than sites at harmonic midpoints
(\clusterNodeApMean{} vs.\ \clusterAntiApMean{} neighbors;
$\clusterNodeVsAntiApRatio\times$, $p = \clusterNodeVsAntiApMWp$).

\paragraph*{Subtest: does clustering produce the unit-sensitivity peak?}
\label{sec:cluster_cond_sensitivity}
A joint permutation test (\clusterCondN{} iterations, two independent shuffles
per trial) asks whether extreme clustering mechanically produces the
canonical-unit peak. Of \clusterCondClusterOk{} clustering-matched
permutations ($p_\mathrm{cluster} = 0.0036$), the conditional fraction
producing the peak is \clusterCondPeakFrac{}, nearly identical to the
unconditional rate of \clusterCondPeakFracUncond{}. The unit-sensitivity
peak and the clustering signal are statistically independent; the primary
evidence remains the clustering (Test~3, $p = \clusterPermP$) and dome
enrichment (Test~2, $p = \simDomePermP$).

\paragraph*{Methodological note: Rayleigh phase-lock (Test~B).}
\label{sec:results_rayleigh}
The near-perfect Rayleigh $R$ of the A+ sub-sample is a mathematical
consequence of the A+ classification criterion (\S\ref{sec:circularity})
and is reported as a structural description, not independent evidence.
A permutation check confirms that a random draw of \Nap{} longitudes from
the full corpus does not reproduce the observed phase concentration
($R = \rayleighR$, $Z = \rayleighZ$, $p_\mathrm{perm} < 0.0001$,
10{,}000 shuffles, seed~42).
The full corpus Rayleigh~$R = \fullRayleighR$
($p_\mathrm{perm} = \fullRayleighPermP$, ns) confirms no spurious global
periodicity in the raw longitude file.
Full details: \texttt{x18\_periodicity\_formal\_test.py}.

\subsection{Global Enrichment (Test~1, Descriptive)}
\label{sec:results_global}

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth]{figures/fig_devhist}
\caption{Distribution of beru deviations for the full UNESCO corpus.
  The excess of sites within the Tier-A+ window ($\delta \leq 0.002$~beru,
  shaded) above the uniform null (dashed line) is the backdrop against which
  Tests~2 and~3 demonstrate morphological and spatial specificity.}
\label{fig:devhist}
\end{figure}

The full UNESCO Cultural/Mixed corpus ($N = \NclusterTotal$) shows a
Tier-A+ rate of \clusterApRate\%
(\NclusterAp/\NclusterTotal; Clopper-Pearson 95\% CI [4.2\%, 7.1\%]),
above the 4\% null
(binomial $p = \clusterApBinom$; Bonferroni reference at $k = 2$:
$p_\mathrm{adj} = \pAdjTestOne$; descriptive only).
Both Gerizim (external) and Jerusalem (inscribed, self-excluded to
$N = 1010$) produce $p = 0.010$; the signal belongs to the
$\sim$35\textdegree{}E corridor, not to either anchor individually
(\S\ref{sec:sensitivity_global}).
Test~1 provides the corpus-level backdrop for the stronger morphological
and spatial results in Tests~2 and~3.

The full-corpus enrichment ratio (1.38$\times$) is modest: approximately
15 additional A+ sites above chance expectation in 1011, consistent with a
scenario in which only a fraction of sites were placed with reference to a
grid. The dome ($3.06\times$; 95\% CI [1.6$\times$, 5.2$\times$]) and stupa
($5.56\times$) sub-populations show stronger effect sizes, with the caveat
that the stupa corpus ($N = \NevoStupa$) has correspondingly wide CIs.

\textbf{Region-stratified robustness.}
Mantel-Haenszel analysis by UNESCO region: OR~$= \MHcommonOR$,
$p = \pCMH$ (trend-level). Tested against region-specific empirical null
rates, enrichment remains significant ($p = 0.0013$; pre-2000: $p = 0.0076$).

\subsubsection*{World religion sites}

Sites associated with any world religion ($N = \NreligUnion$): \religUnionApRate\%
A+ ($\religUnionEnrich\times$, $p = \pReligUnion$~*).
Christianity ($N = \NchristSites$): \christApRate\% A+, $p = \pChrist$~*.
Buddhism ($N = 46$): 8.7\% A+ ($p = 0.11$, ns).
These populations were defined by religion-name matching only.

\noindent The A+ rate is highest in the founding canon (1978--1984: \canonApRate\%,

\textbf{Five-cohort gradient} (Table~\ref{tab:fivecohort}). Only the founding
canon reaches individual significance ($p < 0.05$); the 1992--1999 cohort is
marginal ($p = 0.064$). Post-2000 cohorts are indistinguishable from the 4\%
null. The 1985--1991 cohort breaks the otherwise monotonic decline, likely
reflecting a disproportionate share of Mixed-category sites in that period.

\begin{table}[H]

Cohort & $N$ & A+ & Rate & Enrichment & $p$ (binomial) \\

1985--1991 & \NcohortB & \NcohortBap & \cohortBrate\% & $1.42\times$ & 0.205~ns \\

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth]{figures/fig_temporal}
\caption{Tier-A+ rate declines monotonically with UNESCO inscription
  year.
  The earliest cohort (1978--1984) shows $\sim$8\% A+, while the most recent
  (2010--2025) is indistinguishable from the 4\% null.
  Cochran-Armitage trend test: $Z = \ZcochranThree$,
  $p = \pCochranThree$ (three-cohort, one-sided).}
\label{fig:temporal}
\end{figure}

\textbf{Cochran-Armitage trend test.}
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Three-cohort:} $Z = \ZcochranThree$, $p = \pCochranThree$~(*), one-sided.
  \item \textbf{Five-cohort:} $Z = \ZcochranFive$, $p = \pCochranFive$~(*), one-sided.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Regional composition confound.}
The founding canon is concentrated in Europe and the Mediterranean.
A region-stratified diagnostic (\texttt{regional\_temporal\_gradient.py})
finds \stratNconcordant{} of \stratNregions{} regions showing the
early~$>$~late direction, but \stratNsig{} reach individual significance.
The Mantel-Haenszel region-adjusted OR is $\MHcommonOR$
($\chi^2 = \CMHchisq$, $p = \pCMH$). The temporal gradient is largely
explained by regional composition and is treated as descriptive only.

\textbf{Running-cumulative stability.}
The cumulative A+ rate reaches $p < 0.05$ by $N = \firstSigN$
(through \firstSigYear) and remains significant ($p < 0.05$) through the full corpus.
Peak $-\!\log_{10}(p)$ occurs at $N = \peakSigN$ (through \peakSigYear),
identified post-hoc (Table~\ref{tab:sequential}).

  (through \peakSigYear); full-corpus significance persists but at
  reduced strength.

\label{tab:sequential}

1993                       & 324 & 24 & 7.4\% & 0.003~** & 2.50 \\

\noindent Enrichment is concentrated in the first half of the corpus
($\leq$2000: \firstHalfRate\%, $p = \pFirstHalf$;
2001--2025: \secondHalfRate\%, $p = \pSecondHalf$~ns).
The sequential peak is post-hoc and classified as exploratory.

\subsubsection*{Line 2b: Founding-era dating (supplementary context)}
\label{sec:resultsfounding_origin}

Founding-era estimates were extracted from UNESCO XML descriptions for
\NdatedSites{} of \NoriginCorpus{} sites
(code: \texttt{deep\_temporal\_analysis.py}).
The BCE combined bin shows directional elevation ($p = \pPreCEbinom$, ns;
underpowered). The early-modern bin (1500--1800~CE: \earlyModernRate\%,
$p = \pEarlyModern$~*) is the only individually significant era but is
not predicted by the beru hypothesis and is flagged as anomalous.
Canon-inscribed post-CE sites show \canonPostCErate\% A+
($p = \pCanonPostCE$~**, $N = \canonPostCEN$).
The key finding is that inscription year is a stronger predictor of A+
status than founding era.

\noindent\textit{Founding-site classifier (Test~E, post-hoc;
Appendix~\ref{sec:kwdaudit_founding}).}
A founding-site classifier identifies A+ sites associated with
founding capitals and sacred origins at an elevated rate
(Fisher OR~$= \foundKwFisherOR$, $p = \pFoundKwFisher$~*).
Because the classifier was refined after seeing the data, it is
classified as fully post-hoc and removed from the main evidential chain.
The two primary results (Tests~2 and~3) are unaffected.

\noindent
The signal is present in the full corpus ($p = \pOriginAll$),
concentrated in religion-associated sites ($p = \pReligUnion$) and
the earliest cohort ($p = \pCanon$), and absent in the modern era
($p = \pModern$~ns).

\subsection{Temporal Gradient (Test~4, Descriptive)}
\label{sec:resultstemporal}

A within-region diagnostic was computed for each of \stratNregions{}
UNESCO regions before examining the pooled Cochran-Armitage result.
Of \stratNregions{} regions, \stratNconcordant{} show the
early-greater-than-late direction, but \stratNsig{} reach individual
significance. The Mantel-Haenszel region-adjusted OR is $\MHcommonOR$
($\chi^2 = \CMHchisq$, $p = \pCMH$). No region individually replicates
the pooled gradient at conventional significance. The temporal gradient
is therefore treated as descriptive only because regional composition is
the dominant driver.

The pooled Cochran-Armitage trend test yields
$Z = \ZcochranThree$, $p = \pCochranThree$ (three-cohort, one-sided;
five-cohort $Z = \ZcochranFive$, $p = \pCochranFive$).

The substantive observation is that UNESCO's own ordering of importance
correlates with the beru-harmonic signal: the earliest-inscribed cohort
(1978--1984, \canonApRate\%, $\canonEnrich\times$) shows the highest A+
rate, while the most recent cohort (2010--2025, \cohortErate\%) is
indistinguishable from the null. This is interpretively coherent: if a
longitude grid influenced the siting of cosmographically important
ancient foundations, then the sites UNESCO identifies as paramount are
precisely those the hypothesis predicts would fall on harmonic nodes.
The within-region adjustment shows this pattern is substantially
explained by geography, but that geographic explanation is itself part
of what the beru-alignment hypothesis predicts: the grid is Eurasian in
origin, so its strongest signal should be in the ancient Eurasian
heartland. The gradient does not adjudicate between geographic
concentration and metrological readings, but is qualitatively consistent
with the hypothesis. Full cohort tables and sequential analysis are in
\S\ref{sec:resultsfounding_origin}.

\section{Robustness Checks}
\label{sec:robustness}

Primary pre-specified results: dome/spherical (Test~2, Bonferroni
$p_\mathrm{adj} = \pAdjTestTwo$, $k = 2$) and cluster asymmetry
(Test~3, Bonferroni $p_\mathrm{adj} = \pAdjTestThree$, $k = 2$);
both exploratory without pre-registration. Test~1 (descriptive) and
Test~4 (descriptive after within-region adjustment) are secondary.

\subsection{Unit Sensitivity Sweep}
\label{sec:metrology}

Varying harmonic spacing from 0.05 to 0.20~beru (physical threshold
fixed at 6.7~km), the 0.1-beru spacing produces the strongest joint
significance across both the dome and full-corpus populations
(Table~\ref{tab:unitsweep}). The 0.05-beru and 0.20-beru spacings also
reach significance, as expected structurally: every 0.10-beru harmonic is
also a 0.05-beru harmonic ($0.10 = 2 \times 0.05$), and 0.20-beru harmonics
are a strict subset. Adjacent non-harmonic spacings (0.06, 0.07, 0.09, 0.11,
0.12~beru) are non-significant in both populations, which is the
discriminating observation.

\textbf{Note on the 0.08-beru row.}
The 0.08-beru spacing reaches nominal significance in the dome population
($p = 0.023$) but not in the full corpus ($p = 0.437$, ns). Inspection
shows 8 of 9 dome hits are a near-complete subset of the 11 A+ dome sites
under the 0.10-beru grid, reflecting unsystematic local overlap between the
two grids. This is most likely a small-$N$ sampling artifact.

\subsection{Sensitivity Slope at the Canonical Unit}
\label{sec:sensitivity_slope_perm}

A fine sweep within $\pm 1\%$ of 0.10~beru (Table~\ref{tab:unitsweep_fine})
shows that a $-0.5\%$ shift (0.0995~beru) collapses the dome population to
non-significance, and a $\pm 0.3\%$ shift collapses both populations.
Only the canonical value and its immediate neighbor (0.1001~beru) retain
dome significance. Two permutation tests evaluate this pattern.

\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth]{figures/fig_unitsweep}
\caption{Unit sensitivity: the signal is concentrated at the canonical
  0.1-beru spacing.
  All spacings $\geq 0.3\%$ from canonical collapse to non-significance
  in at least one population; the immediate neighbor (0.1001~beru, $+0.1\%$)
  retains dome significance but loses full-corpus significance.}
\label{fig:unitsweep}
\end{figure}

\textbf{Test I (sharpness permutation).}
\label{sec:sensitivity_slope_perm}
The fine sweep was repeated on \permSlopeNperms{} longitude-permuted datasets
(seed~$= 42$; code: \texttt{sensitivity\_slope\_permutation\_test.py}).
The sharp-peak pattern occurs in \permSlopePsharp{} of permuted datasets
($p_\mathrm{perm} = \permSlopePsharp$, ns). Geographic concentration is
sufficient to reproduce the $\pm 0.3\%$ collapse; this test provides no
independent evidence for the metrological hypothesis.

\textbf{Test II (canonical-unit specificity).}
\label{sec:sensitivity_slope_specificity}
The stronger question is whether 0.1000~beru specifically achieves the best
joint significance. Code: \texttt{sensitivity\_slope\_specificity\allowbreak\_test.py},
\permSlopeSpecN{} permutations. In the observed data, 0.1000~beru ranks
\#\permSlopeCanonRankObs{} of \NfineSweepSpacings{}. The fraction of
permutations where 0.1000 is the unique best joint-significance spacing is
$p = \permSlopeCanonBestJoint{}$ (\permSlopeCanonBestJointSig{}), not
significant. Geographic concentration alone produces the canonical spacing as
the best spacing in approximately \permSlopeCanonBestJoint{} of permutations.

Both permutation tests are non-significant. The unit-sensitivity observation
provides no independent evidential value beyond the dome enrichment and
cluster asymmetry. The 0.1-beru harmonic interval derives from the documented
sexagesimal subdivision of the Babylonian beru (\S\ref{sec:metrology_basis}).

\subsection{Global Anchor Sweep}
\label{sec:sensitivity_global}

Two parallel sweeps (0--360\textdegree{}E, 0.01\textdegree{} resolution,
\anchorSweepNanchors{} trial anchors each) assess whether the
$\sim$35\textdegree{}E corridor is unusual globally.
Sweep~A removes Jerusalem and tests Gerizim as anchor ($N = 1010$);
Sweep~B retains Jerusalem with self-exclusion ($N = 1010$).

Both anchors place the corridor in the global top 3\%, and the result
is anchor-invariant within the \GerizimJerusalemKm\,km corridor window.
The single-site difference between sweeps (55 vs.\ 56 A+) is precisely
the Old City of Jerusalem ($\delta = 0.0012$~beru from Gerizim's
harmonic). No other historical meridian reaches significance except
Mecca (\MeccaAp{} A+, $p = \pMeccaAnchor$~$^\dagger$).

\subsubsection*{The $x.18$\textdegree{}E periodic structure}
\label{sec:x18artifact}

The most striking result of the global sweep is that every longitude of
the form $x.18$\textdegree{}E (all 120 such anchors) produces identically
the maximum A+ count. The spacing between consecutive $x.18$\textdegree{}E
anchors is exactly 3\textdegree{} = 0.1~beru.

\medskip
\noindent\fbox{%
\begin{minipage}{0.93\textwidth}
\small
\textbf{Note on the ``$x.18$\textdegree{}E'' phase label.}
The label is a rounded shorthand for the phase value of approximately
2.18\textdegree{} mod 3.0\textdegree{}. Mount Gerizim's longitude
mod 3.0\textdegree{} is $\GerizimLon \bmod 3.0 = \GerizimPhase$\textdegree{}
(phase gap $\phaseGap$\textdegree{}, $\approx \phaseGapKm$~km), well
within combined coordinate and band-width uncertainty.
\end{minipage}%
}
\medskip

Any site near the $x.18$\textdegree{} phase can serve as a high-scoring
anchor. The choice of Gerizim was made on historical grounds
(\S\ref{sec:anchor}), and the corridor result is anchor-invariant
within the band.

\textbf{Non-independence.} The 3\textdegree{} periodicity and the unit
sensitivity sweep (\S\ref{sec:metrology}) are two views of a single
pattern and do not constitute independent evidence.

Two interpretations apply.
\textit{Interpretation~1 (beru-alignment):} A global grid with
3\textdegree{} spacing anchored to the $\sim$35\textdegree{}E corridor
would produce exactly this pattern.
\textit{Interpretation~2 (geographic concentration):} Eurasian heritage
sites cluster in longitude bands that happen to align with the
$x.18$\textdegree{} phase. This cannot be refuted on the UNESCO corpus
alone. The unit sensitivity (\S\ref{sec:metrology}) is the observation
that most constrains this interpretation.

Code: \texttt{peak\_geography\_audit.py}, \texttt{verify\_x18\_periodicity.py}.

\subsubsection*{Formal statistical test for the $x.18$\textdegree{} periodicity}
\label{sec:x18_formal}

Three permutation tests (10,000 shuffles, seed~42;
code: \texttt{analysis/global/\allowbreak x18\_periodicity\_formal\_test.py}):

\textbf{Test~A (full-corpus Rayleigh~$R$, mod~3\textdegree{}).}
$R = \fullRayleighR$, $Z = \fullRayleighZ$,
$p_\mathrm{perm} = \fullRayleighPermP$ (ns). No spurious global
periodicity in the raw longitude distribution.

\textbf{Test~B (A+-only Rayleigh~$R$).}
Because A+ sites are classified by the 0.002-beru threshold, near-perfect
$R$ is structurally expected and does not independently confirm periodicity.
The permutation null (random draw of \Nap{} longitudes) does not reproduce
the observed concentration: $R = \rayleighR$, $Z = \rayleighZ$,
$p_\mathrm{perm} = \rayleighPermP$ ($p < 0.0001$).

\textbf{Test~C (circular-shift anchor null).}
A randomly placed anchor recovers only $\sim\!\anchorShiftNullMu$ A+ on
average versus \anchorShiftApCount{} at Gerizim
($Z = \anchorShiftZ$, $p_\mathrm{perm} = \anchorShiftPermP$, *).

Together, these tests confirm that the 3\textdegree{} structure is a real
property of the corpus and that the $x.18$\textdegree{}E phase is
specifically aligned with the data.

Code: \texttt{analysis/global/\allowbreak x18\_periodicity\_formal\_test.py}.
\label{sec:dome_periodicity}

\textbf{Dome-specific periodicity audit}
(\texttt{analysis/global/dome\_periodicity\_audit.py}).

\textit{Part~1 (dome-specific anchor sweep):}
Gerizim scores at the \domeGerizimPctile\textsuperscript{th} percentile
among dome sites, tying for the global maximum.

\textit{Part~2 (phase distribution):}
Dome site phases are approximately uniform ($\chi^2 = \domePhaseChiSq$,
df\,=\,9, $p = \domePhaseChiP$, trend-level) and consistent with the
full corpus (KS $D = \domePhaseKSD$, $p = \domePhaseKSP$, ns).

\textit{Parts~3--4 (concentration within the phase window):}
All \NcircTierAp{} dome A+ sites have phases within $\pm 0.15$\textdegree{}
of the $x.18$\textdegree{} optimal phase, as expected if dome sites were
placed on the 0.1-beru grid.

\textit{Part~5 (morphological specificity within the band):}
Within the $x.18$\textdegree{} window, dome sites show
\domeBandRate\%{} A+ ($N = \domeBandN$) and non-dome sites show
\nonDomeBandRate\%{} A+ ($N = \nonDomeBandN$; Fisher
OR~$= \domeBandFisherOR$, $p = \domeBandFisherP$, ns). Both groups are
enriched within the band, consistent with both the geographic-concentration
and beru-alignment readings.

\textbf{Assessment.}
The audit is consistent with the beru-alignment hypothesis at every stage
but equally consistent with geographic concentration. The dome enrichment
surviving all three simulation nulls remains the primary evidence against
a purely geographic account.

\textbf{The $\sim$35\textdegree{}E corridor.}
Jerusalem and Gerizim are \GerizimJerusalemKm\,km apart.
Against the full corpus, Gerizim scores 56~A+; with Jerusalem removed,
55~A+ (the $\pm1$ is Jerusalem itself, $\delta = 0.0012$~beru). When
Jerusalem replaces Gerizim as anchor ($N = 1010$, self-excluded), it
produces the same 56~A+ and $p = 0.010$. The enrichment belongs to the
corridor, not to the specific anchor.

\textbf{Landmark ranking.}
Using each of the \NclusterTotal{} UNESCO sites as anchor, Gerizim%
\footnote{Anchor coordinate (\GerizimLon\textdegree{}E) from Palestine's
UNESCO tentative list (ref.~5706, 2012). Gerizim is not on the inscribed
list and does not appear in the corpus. Full citation and archived URL in
\S\ref{sec:anchor}.}
ranks \#\GerizimTempleRank{} (\GerizimTempleAp{} A+ sites, top 4.1\%).
Excluding sites at the $x.18$\textdegree{}E phase, Gerizim ranks \#24
of 965 (top 2.5\%).

\textbf{Global corridor comparison (Table~\ref{tab:corridors}, post-hoc).}
The 35\textdegree{}E corridor leads globally with \corridorJeruN{} sites
scoring $\geq 55$ A+. The closest rival (23.2\textdegree{}E, $N=4$) spans
four countries with no shared tradition.

\subsection{Spatial Autocorrelation}
\label{sec:autocorrelation}

A+ harmonics host $\clusterHarmonicRatio\times$ as many total UNESCO
sites as non-A+ harmonics ($p < 0.0001$).

\subsubsection*{Effective-$N$ correction}

DEFF via ICC ($\pm 0.5$\textdegree{}, $\rho = \ICChalf$):
DEFF~$= \DEFFhalf$, $N_\mathrm{eff} = \NeffHalf$,
corrected $p = \pNeffHalf$.
Block bootstrap (3\textdegree{} blocks, 100{,}000 resamples):
95\% CI $[\blockBootCIlo\%, \blockBootCIhi\%]$ excludes the 4\% null.

\subsubsection*{Region-conditioned permutation}

A within-group permutation (\regionCondNperms{} trials, seed~42;
\texttt{region\_conditioned\_\allowbreak permutation.py}) tested whether geographic
concentration of heritage sites in beru-aligned longitude bands drives the
enrichment.
Strategy~A grouped the \regionCondPermObs{} A+ sites by UNESCO
administrative region crossed with 20\textdegree{} longitude bin
(\regionCondNregions{} cells); Strategy~B used 5\textdegree{} global bins
(\regionCondBNblocks{} blocks).
In both cases every trial reproduced the
observed A+ count exactly (permutation mean $= \regionCondPermMean$,
$\mathrm{SD} = \regionCondPermStd$).
This is mathematically inevitable: a
bin of $\geq 5$\textdegree{} spans at least one full 3\textdegree{} harmonic
period, so shuffling within the bin leaves each site's harmonic residue
unchanged and A+ membership is structurally invariant.
Within-bin permutation therefore cannot discriminate a real alignment from geographic
concentration at this resolution.
The geographic-concentration question is
addressed by the dedicated Null~A and Null~C simulations
(\S\ref{sec:geo_conc_null}), which show that proximity to harmonic longitudes
is necessary but not sufficient ($p = \geoNullDomeRestrictedP$,~**)
to explain the dome enrichment.

\subsection{Null Model Validation}
\label{sec:simulation_null}

Three simulation nulls (100{,}000 trials each, seed~$= 42$,
\citealt{good2005permutation}):

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Longitude permutation.}
    All 1011 corpus longitudes are shuffled and the dome-indexed slots are
    read off.  This tests whether dome sites are more enriched than expected
    given the full-corpus longitude distribution (including its Eurasian
    concentration).
    Dome ($N = \NcircValidated$, context-validated): $p_\mathrm{perm} = \simDomePermP$~(**).
    Canon ($N = 138$): $p = 0.14$~(ns).
    Pre-2000 ($N = 501$): $p = 0.074$~($^\dagger$).
  \item \textbf{Bootstrap from full corpus.}
    Draw $N = \NcircValidated$ from the full 1011-site longitude distribution.
    Dome: $p_\mathrm{boot} = \simDomeBootP$~(*).
  \item \textbf{KDE-smoothed null (full corpus).}
    Expected $40.4 \pm 6.2$, observed 56, $Z = 2.50$, $p = 0.011$~(*).
\end{itemize}

Dome enrichment survives all three models.
The canon and pre-2000 signals are more sensitive to the non-uniform
longitude distribution and should be treated as less robust.

\textbf{Geographic-concentration null (dedicated simulation).}
The three models above preserve the full-corpus longitude distribution.
The dedicated geographic-concentration test (\S\ref{sec:geo_conc_null};
\texttt{dome\_geographic\_concentration\_test.py}) goes further by testing
whether the dome enrichment can be explained by the geographic positions
of dome sites \emph{themselves}.

Null~A (within-dome bootstrap) resamples from the dome sites' own longitudes:
$p = \geoNullDomeBootP$ (ns), bootstrap mean $= \geoNullDomeBootMean$~A+.
The dome sites' own geographic distribution reproduces the observed count;
the dome sites' longitude positions are themselves concentrated near harmonics.

Null~C (restricted geographic draw) asks whether any monument from the dome
longitude footprint shows comparable enrichment: pool of \geoNullDomeRestrictedN{}
full-corpus sites within $\pm 5$\textdegree{} of any dome site, mean A+ $= \geoNullDomeRestrictedMean$,
$Z = \geoNullDomeRestrictedZ$, $p = \geoNullDomeRestrictedP$ (**).
Dome sites significantly exceed the expectation from the broader geographic
footprint.

Together, Null~A shows \emph{why} dome sites are near harmonics (their longitudes are
concentrated there), while Null~C establishes that \emph{other monuments at those
same longitudes} are significantly less enriched.
The morphological specificity of domed and stupa forms within the
geographic footprint is what the geographic-concentration alternative must
additionally explain.

\subsection{Multiple Comparisons}
\label{sec:sharpshooter}

\textbf{Anchor and unit.}
Gerizim ranks at the \anchorSweepPctile{}\textsuperscript{th} percentile;
Jerusalem produces the same 56~A+ ($p = 0.010$) from the same corridor
(global top~3\%). A $\pm 0.3\%$ unit shift collapses joint significance.

\textbf{Tier thresholds.}
The A+ window ($\delta \leq 0.002$~beru, 4\% null) was fixed before any
beru values were computed.

\textbf{Keyword selection.}
Full-corpus Test~3 requires no keywords. The meta-keyword test
(\NmetaKeywords{} candidates) finds only 44 (2.5\%) matching the curated
set's enrichment.

\textbf{Number of tests.}
Primary pre-specified family: $k = 2$ (Tests~2 and~3;
\S\ref{sec:bonferroni}). All results are exploratory. Test~E is post-hoc.
FDR audit (\NtotalTests{} tests) retains \NsurviveFDR{} at $q < 0.05$;
\NsurviveBonfAll{} survive Bonferroni at $k = \NtotalTests$.

\textbf{Framework choice.}
Longitude-only framework selected as the most parsimonious test;
noted as a design constraint.

\section{Limitations}
\label{sec:limitations}

\textbf{Keyword selection.}
Test~2 uses a mechanical morphological filter (audit: \S\ref{sec:kwdaudit}).
No site was excluded based on its beru deviation. Test~3 requires no keywords.

\textbf{Non-uniform publication density.}
The simulation null (\S\ref{sec:simulation_null}) preserves the observed
longitude distribution; any density gradient affects A+ and non-A+ sites
equally.

\textbf{Anchor corridor.}
Both anchors produce $p \approx 0.010$ (raw binomial; Test~1, descriptive
only). The corridor is stable at 56~A+ from either vantage point (global
top~3\%; \S\ref{sec:sensitivity_global}). Gerizim was selected on the
basis of the \textit{tabbur ha-aretz} tradition
\citep{macdonald1964,crown1989}; no optimization across alternative anchors
was performed. Jerusalem produces the same result, confirming corridor-level
invariance.

\textbf{Founding-site classifier.}
Test~E uses a post-hoc classifier (Appendix~\ref{sec:kwdaudit_founding}).
This has no effect on the primary results (Tests~2 and~3).

\textbf{Spatial autocorrelation.}
DEFF correction: $N_\mathrm{eff} = \NeffHalf$ (DEFF $= \DEFFhalf$,
corrected $p = \pNeffHalf$). Block bootstrap CI
$[\blockBootCIlo\%, \blockBootCIhi\%]$ excludes the 4\% null.

\textbf{Americas as negative control.}
The Americas sub-corpus ($N = 144$, A+ = 2.1\%, ns) shows directional
depletion consistent with a Eurasian-anchored grid. One-sided binomial:
$p = \AmericasOneSidedP$ (\AmericasOneSidedStar). The Wikidata P1435
Americas control ($N = \AmericasCtrlN$, A+ = \AmericasCtrlRate\%) also
shows depletion.

\textbf{Geocoding precision.}
UNESCO XML coordinates represent administrative centroids, introducing
unsystematic noise but not directional bias. The dome enrichment is
robust across raw ($N = 90$, $p = 0.0009$) and context-validated
($N = 83$, $p = 0.0005$) populations and survives all three simulation
nulls. A formal coordinate-perturbation analysis is deferred to future work.

\textbf{Geographic and cultural selection bias.}
The UNESCO list over-represents Europe and the Mediterranean-to-South-Asian
corridor. This is the most important alternative explanation. Any
site-dense longitude band will produce elevated hit rates under a periodic
grid. The $x.18$\textdegree{}E structure is a real property of the
UNESCO longitude distribution, and approximately 3\% of arbitrary anchors
produce comparable A+ counts.

The geographic-concentration and metrological readings accept the same
geographic facts but disagree on whether the quantization at 0.1-beru
precision is deliberate. Permutation tests of the sensitivity slope
(\S\ref{sec:sensitivity_slope_perm}, \S\ref{sec:sensitivity_slope_specificity})
show that geographic concentration alone can reproduce both the sharpness
and location of the sensitivity peak. The dome enrichment surviving
simulation nulls, particularly the restricted geographic draw (Null~C,
$p = \geoNullDomeRestrictedP$, **), remains the primary evidence against
a purely geographic account: dome sites significantly exceed the enrichment
of other monuments at the same longitudes.

\textbf{Wikidata P1435 control.}
The P1435 corpus ($N = \WDctrlN$) serves as broad external reference.
Because P1435 includes structures of all significance levels with a
different geographic distribution, it cannot serve as a direct null model.
For transparency: OR~$= \WDctrlOR$ [95\% CI: \WDctrlORlo--\WDctrlORhi],
$p = \WDctrlZp$; MH OR~$= \WDctrlMHOR$, $p = \WDctrlMHp$. These are
reported as descriptive context only.

\textbf{UNESCO listing and non-metrological correlates.}
Historical significance partially correlates with the tested predictors.
The dome enrichment surviving simulation nulls is the primary evidence
against purely non-metrological explanations.

\textbf{Temporal gradient.}
The gradient ($p = \pCochranThree$; descriptive only) could reflect a
UNESCO curation artifact. After within-region adjustment (MH OR = $\MHcommonOR$,
$p = \pCMH$), the gradient is largely explained by regional composition.

\textbf{Global 3\textdegree{} periodicity.}%
\label{sec:xdot18_artifact}
The UNESCO longitude distribution contains a real 3\textdegree{} periodic
structure whose phase is anchored near Gerizim and whose spacing matches
the 0.1-beru unit. This may reflect an ancient surveying grid or geographic
concentration of Eurasian civilizations. Gerizim's phase
(2.269\textdegree{} mod~3.0\textdegree{}) differs from the optimal by
\phaseGap\textdegree{} ($\sim$\phaseGapKm~km), within normal coordinate
uncertainty.

\textbf{Metrological evidence.}
No archaeological instrument calibrated in beru subdivisions has been
recovered. The evidence for beru-based angular practice is entirely
documentary
\citep{hunger2019mul,robson2008mathematics,powell1987metrological}.
The beru hypothesis rests on the textual and statistical evidence
presented here.

%──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\section{Discussion}
\label{sec:discussion}

\subsection{Summary of Findings}

The central result is an apparent global 3\textdegree{} periodic structure
in the UNESCO longitude distribution. Every anchor of the form
$x.18$\textdegree{}E produces the maximum observed A+ count. The spacing
matches the metrologically attested 0.1-beru unit. Sub-percent shifts
($\pm 0.3\%$) collapse significance in both the full corpus and the dome
sub-population, but permutation tests show this collapse is not unusual
under the empirical null (\S\ref{sec:sensitivity_slope_perm},
\S\ref{sec:sensitivity_slope_specificity}). Unit sensitivity is consistent
with but does not independently confirm the metrological hypothesis.

The Levantine corridor ($\sim$35\textdegree{}E) is one of 120 equally
phase-matched windows. The historical motivation for starting from this
corridor predates the discovery of the periodicity, but the periodicity
is a property of the corpus, not an independent finding of the anchor.

Domed and spherical monuments ($N = \NcircTotal$, \NcircApRate\% A+;
Test~2: permutation $Z = \simDomePermZ$, $p = \simDomePermP$; Bonferroni
$p_\mathrm{adj} = \pAdjTestTwo$, $k = 2$) show concentrated alignment.
A+-bearing harmonics host $\clusterHarmonicRatio\times$ more sites than
non-A+ harmonics ($p < 0.0001$, permutation $p = \clusterPermP$; Test~3).
The stupa stage shows the highest enrichment ($5.56\times$,
$p = \pEvoStupa$, $N = \NevoStupa$) but warrants caution given the small
corpus. The A+ rate declines from the earliest inscription cohort
(\canonApRate\%) to the most recent (\cohortErate\%), largely explained
by regional composition (\S\ref{sec:resultstemporal}).

\subsection{What the Results Do and Do Not Show}

The results document a reproducible quantitative pattern: a 3\textdegree{}
periodic structure whose phase aligns with the Levantine corridor and
whose spacing matches the 0.1-beru unit. The dome enrichment survives the
$k=2$ multiple-comparisons correction, simulation nulls, and
spatial-autocorrelation adjustment. Determining the mechanism requires
evidence outside the scope of this paper.

\subsection{Alternative Explanations}
\label{sec:alt_explanations}

\textbf{(1) Independent convergence.}
Hemispherical structures are a recurring architectural solution, but
independent convergence predicts no consistent longitude specificity or
global periodicity.

\textbf{(2) Engineering necessity.}
Terrain or resource access may attract construction at certain longitudes,
but no global periodicity at a specific angular spacing is predicted.

\textbf{(3) UNESCO curation and geographic concentration.}
This is the strongest alternative and cannot be excluded on this corpus
alone. Permutation tests show geographic concentration can reproduce the
sensitivity-collapse pattern (\S\ref{sec:sensitivity_slope_perm}).
However, the restricted geographic draw (Null~C,
$p = \geoNullDomeRestrictedP$, **) shows that other monuments from the
same longitude footprint are significantly less enriched than dome sites.
The geographic-concentration account must explain why domed and stupa
forms specifically are disproportionately at harmonic sub-positions.

\textbf{(4) Random coincidence.}
Three simulation approaches fail to reproduce the dome enrichment
(permutation $Z = \simDomePermZ$, $p = \simDomePermP$; bootstrap
$p = \simDomeBootP$).

\textbf{Working assessment.}
Three observations constitute the core pattern:
(1)~a global 3\textdegree{} periodic structure aligned with the Levantine
corridor and matching the 0.1-beru unit (descriptive, Test~1);
(2)~morphological specificity (dome enrichment surviving simulation nulls;
Test~2); and (3)~cluster asymmetry ($\clusterHarmonicRatio\times$,
$p = \clusterPermP$; Test~3). The geographic-concentration alternative
remains viable because the distribution of civilizations and beru
harmonics from the Levant are not independent. This study offers the
result as a quantitative basis for further investigation.

\subsection{Directions for Future Work}

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Independent replication using a non-UNESCO heritage corpus.
  \item Regional sub-analyses for Mesoamerican, North American, and Australian corpora.
  \item Textual and archaeological evidence for Levantine-anchored angular
    reference frames (cuneiform, Sanskrit, Hellenistic geographic sources).
  \item Extension to the latitude dimension.
  \item Dome enrichment stratified by founding-site category using an
    independently assembled founding-site list.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{Conclusion}

This study tested whether UNESCO World Heritage site longitudes encode a
0.1-beru harmonic grid anchored in the Levant, motivated by cuneiform
evidence for the Babylonian beru as a unit of geographic arc.
All results are exploratory (\S\ref{sec:circularity}).

The UNESCO longitude distribution contains a global 3\textdegree{} periodic
structure (Test~1, descriptive). Sub-percent shifts from the canonical
0.1-beru value collapse significance. The dome sub-population (Test~2)
survives all three simulation nulls, and A+-bearing harmonics host
$\clusterHarmonicRatio\times$ more sites than non-A+ harmonics (Test~3,
$p = \clusterPermP$). Multiple-comparisons bounds are applied to the
$k = 2$ pre-specified family.

These findings admit a geographic-concentration alternative.
Permutation tests show the sensitivity peak is reproducible under an
empirical null. The geographic-concentration account must then explain why
the longitude clustering of the world's most significant monuments
coincides with a metrologically attested unit from ancient Babylonian
astronomy. This paper documents the quantitative regularity; its cause is
an open question.

The two findings most resistant to a purely geographic account are
morphological specificity (dome enrichment exceeding the restricted
geographic draw, Null~C, $p = \geoNullDomeRestrictedP$) and cluster
asymmetry ($p = \clusterPermP$). Together with the global 3\textdegree{}
periodicity, these constitute a coherent exploratory pattern consistent
with a 0.1-beru harmonic grid awaiting independent historical confirmation.

\subsubsection*{Criteria for confirmation}

Independent replication using a non-UNESCO corpus would provide the most
direct test. The hypothesis would gain substantially from: (a)~cuneiform
or other texts explicitly assigning beru-longitude coordinates to distant
sites; (b)~archaeological survey instruments calibrated in beru
sub-divisions; or (c)~textual evidence from Hellenistic, Sanskrit, or
Islamic sources referencing a Levantine-centered angular reference frame.
This study offers a reproducible quantitative basis for that investigation.

\appendix
\newpage

%──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\section{Keyword Audit Summary}
\label{sec:kwdaudit}

This appendix summarizes the audit trail for the dome/spherical monument
filter (Test~2) and the founding-origin classifier (Test~E).
The full site-by-site audit is deposited as online supplementary material
and is reproducible by running:\\
\texttt{python3 analysis/unesco/spherical\_monument\_raw\_sweep.py}\\
\texttt{python3 analysis/unesco/spherical\_monument\_test.py --audit}

\subsection{Dome/Spherical Monument Filter (Test~2 and Test~2x)}
\label{sec:kwdaudit_dome}

\NcircKeywords{} morphological keywords from four roots
(\textit{stupa/stupas}, \textit{tholos}, \textit{dome/domed/domes},
\textit{spherical}) are matched as whole words against each site's
name, XML description, and extended OUV text.

\textbf{Test~2 (primary):} All \NcircTotal{} matching sites are included
without context gating. This raw positive sweep uses a population fully
determined by the keyword list, with no post-hoc filtering decisions.

\textbf{Test~2x (exploratory robustness check):} Unambiguous keywords
are accepted without validation. Ambiguous keywords (\textit{dome, domed,
domes, spherical}) are subject to a two-gate context check: Gate~1
(28 negative patterns) rejects non-architectural uses; Gate~2
(86 positive patterns) requires architectural confirmation. Full gate
lists: \texttt{lib/dome\_filter.py}.

Of the \NcircTotal{} raw-sweep sites, \NcircContextRejected{} are
rejected by context validation in Test~2x (Hiroshima Peace Memorial,
Richtersveld, Gyeongju, Vi\~{n}ales Valley, Rock Islands Southern Lagoon,
Uluru-Kata Tju\d{t}a, and Diqu\'{i}s Stone Spheres). In every case, the
exclusion is driven by the semantic domain of the keyword match, not by
any property of the site's longitude. Test~2x yields $N = 83$,
$p = 0.0005$, $3.31\times$ enrichment, confirming that the 7 rejected
sites are longitude-diluting false positives.

\subsection{Founding-Origin Classifier (Test~E, Post-Hoc)}
\label{sec:kwdaudit_founding}

The founding-origin classifier (\texttt{lib/founding\_filter.py})
assigns sites to five categories: Founding Capital (F), Sacred Origin
(S), Founding Monument (M), Founding Axis (X), and Ancient Continuous
Landscape (L), using unambiguous and context-gated keywords. Full
keyword lists: \texttt{config.json}.

This classifier was iteratively tuned after data inspection and is
classified as fully post-hoc. It carries no weight in the primary
evidential chain but addresses a substantive question: what kinds of
sites, beyond domed and stupa monuments, tend to fall on
$x.18$\textdegree{}E nodes? The classifier finds enrichment not only
in morphologically domed structures but in sites associated with the
founding of civilizational and religious traditions.

\textbf{Keyword enrichment result.}
Applied to the full corpus ($N = \NoriginCorpus$), the classifier finds
\NfoundKwAp{} keyword-founding sites among the 56 A+ sites
(\foundKwApRate\%; $N_{\mathrm{kw}} = \NfoundKwAll$, base rate
\foundKwBaseRate\%). Fisher exact test: OR~$= \foundKwFisherOR$,
$p = \pFoundKwFisher$~*. The complete version history is preserved in
the project's \texttt{git} log at
\url{https://github.com/SethTenenbaum/gerizim-paper-a}.

affiliation. The author welcomes correspondence from researchers
interested in replication or collaboration.

All scientific conclusions, hypothesis design, keyword-list decisions,
and analytical choices are solely the author's.
